articles.title: Los Angeles World Cup 2026: The Complete Visitor's Guide
articles.excerpt: Los Angeles hosts 8 FIFA World Cup 2026 matches at SoFi Stadium, including the high-stakes USA vs Paraguay opener on June 12 and Türkiye vs USA on June 25. Here's everything you need to know about getting there, where to stay, and making the most of your time in the city.
articles.content:
Why LA Is One of the Tournament's Best Host Cities

Los Angeles is hosting 8 matches at SoFi Stadium in Inglewood — more than almost any other venue — making it a hub for World Cup action throughout June and into July. With ideal weather, world-class food, and one of the most iconic urban landscapes on the planet, LA rewards visitors who plan ahead. The trick is knowing which neighborhoods to base yourself in and how to move around without losing hours to traffic.

Key Matches to Know

Two matches will draw enormous American crowds: USA vs Paraguay on June 12 and Türkiye vs USA on June 25. Both are expected to sell out fast, and the surrounding days will see intense fan activity across the city. Book accommodation for those dates as early as possible — prices around those weekends are already elevated.

Where to Stay

  • Inglewood (Best for Match Days): Staying within walking distance of SoFi Stadium eliminates all transport headaches. Hotels like the Renaissance Los Angeles Airport Hotel and nearby options put you 5–10 minutes on foot from the gates. It's not the most glamorous neighborhood, but the convenience on match days is unbeatable.
  • Downtown LA: The JW Marriott LA Live sits directly adjacent to the Staples Center area and is a 20-minute Metro ride to the stadium. Downtown gives you easy access to Grand Central Market, the Broad museum, and some of LA's best restaurants.
  • Santa Monica: If you want the beach and the World Cup, Santa Monica delivers. The tradeoff is a longer commute to SoFi — factor in 45–60 minutes via Metro on match days. The vibe is relaxed, the food scene is excellent, and cooling off in the Pacific after a hot afternoon match is genuinely worthwhile.

Getting Around

The Metro C Line (Green Line) is your primary tool. Take it to the Hawthorne/Lennox station, where FIFA will operate free shuttles every 10 minutes directly to SoFi Stadium. The base fare is just $1.75, making this far cheaper and faster than any rideshare option on match days. From LAX, the new Metro Connector to LAX/Metro Transit Center opens June 6, giving you a seamless link from the airport to the C Line — no Uber needed from the airport at all.

Weather and What to Pack

June in LA means temperatures between 72°F and 85°F (22–29°C), with low humidity and consistent sunshine. Afternoon matches will be warm — bring sunscreen, a hat, and a reusable water bottle. SoFi Stadium is an open-air venue with a roof canopy that provides partial shade, but direct sun exposure is real. Dress in layers for evening matches, as coastal breezes can make it cooler after sunset.

Free Fan Zones

Exposition Park, directly adjacent to the Natural History Museum and the LA Memorial Coliseum, is hosting a free FIFA Fan Festival from June 11–15. It features big screens, live music, food vendors, and sponsor activations. No ticket required — just show up. This is the best way to experience World Cup atmosphere in LA without a match ticket, and it doubles as a great pre-match gathering spot if you do have tickets.

Don't Miss

  • Grand Central Market for breakfast or lunch (try Eggslut or Belcampo)
  • The Getty Center for views over the city (free admission, pay only for parking)
  • Venice Beach Boardwalk for a classic LA experience on non-match days
  • Griffith Observatory at sunset — free entry, unbeatable city panorama

LA is a city that rewards those who embrace its sprawl rather than fight it. Plan your match days around the Metro, book accommodation early near Inglewood or Downtown, and leave time to explore beyond the stadium. You won't regret it.

articles.title: Match Day Guide: How to Get to SoFi Stadium for FIFA World Cup 2026
articles.excerpt: Getting to SoFi Stadium on match day requires a plan — traffic around Inglewood locks up hours before kickoff and rideshares surge wildly post-match. Here's a breakdown of every transport option, with honest assessments of each.
articles.content:
The Bottom Line Up Front

The Metro is the best option to reach SoFi Stadium for World Cup matches. Full stop. On high-attendance match days, every other option — driving, Uber, Lyft — involves significant unpredictability that the Metro simply doesn't. Here's the full breakdown of all your choices.

Option 1: Metro C Line (Recommended)

The Metro C Line (Green Line) drops you at Hawthorne/Lennox Station, where FIFA will run free dedicated shuttles to SoFi Stadium every 10 minutes. The whole journey — from most of central LA — takes under 30 minutes and costs just $1.75 each way.

  • From Downtown LA: Take the A Line to Rosa Parks, transfer to the C Line heading west
  • From Hollywood: Take the B Line to 7th/Metro Center, transfer to C Line
  • From the Beach Cities: The C Line runs east–west through the South Bay, making it accessible from Redondo Beach and El Segundo too

Trains run every 6–12 minutes on match days, and FIFA has confirmed extended service hours to cover late finishes. Buy a TAP card at any Metro station (the card itself is $2) and load it with credit — it works on all Metro lines and buses.

Option 2: From LAX (Only 4 Miles Away)

SoFi Stadium is one of the closest major venues to a hub airport in the entire tournament — just 4 miles from LAX. The new LAX/Metro Transit Center, opening June 6, 2026, connects the airport's automated people mover directly to the Metro C Line. From arrival at LAX to the stadium shuttle, you're looking at roughly 25–30 minutes total with no traffic concerns whatsoever. This is a legitimate competitive advantage for LA over most other host cities.

Option 3: Uber / Lyft

Rideshares work well getting to the stadium if you book in advance and leave with plenty of time. The real problem is the return trip. After a 90,000-capacity event ends, every rideshare app in a 5-mile radius surges simultaneously. Prices of $50–90 for a post-match Uber are realistic, and wait times of 30–45 minutes are common.

Critical tip: If you're using a rideshare, book your return trip before the final whistle — ideally 10–15 minutes before the match ends. This dramatically reduces both your wait time and the surge price. The designated rideshare pickup zone at SoFi is on the south side of the stadium.

Option 4: Driving

Driving is viable if you're coming from the South Bay or Orange County, where the surface street approach from the south avoids the worst freeway bottlenecks. Budget for $30–80 in parking depending on how close you want to be, and plan your exit strategy before you arrive. After major matches, expect 60–90 minutes to clear the parking structure and surrounding streets. The SoFi app shows real-time lot availability and allows pre-purchase of reserved parking — worth it if you drive.

Stadium Entry Tips

  • Arrive 2 hours early on major match days (USA games especially) — security lines for 70,000+ crowds can be 30–45 minutes alone
  • SoFi is a cashless stadium — all food, beverages, and merchandise require a card or mobile payment
  • Bring water — June temperatures in Inglewood regularly hit 85°F+ and dehydration is a real risk during afternoon matches
  • Wear sunscreen — the stadium's canopy roof provides partial shade, but many seats get direct afternoon sun
  • Clear bags only are permitted through security (12 x 6 x 12 inches maximum)

What to Do Near the Stadium Before the Match

The immediate Inglewood area has developed considerably since SoFi opened. The Hollywood Park complex surrounding the stadium includes restaurants, a casino, and open plazas — arrive early and explore the pregame atmosphere there rather than waiting in security lines. Food options inside the stadium are solid but expensive; eating nearby beforehand saves both money and time.

articles.title: New York World Cup 2026 Guide: MetLife Stadium and The Final
articles.excerpt: New York is hosting the FIFA World Cup 2026 Final on July 19 at MetLife Stadium — the biggest single sporting event in the world, played in the shadow of Manhattan's skyline. Here's how to experience it, whether you have a ticket or not.
articles.content:
New York's Role in World Cup 2026

Of all 16 host cities, New York carries the most weight. MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey — capacity 82,500 — will host the World Cup Final on July 19, the most-watched sporting event on the planet. But even before the Final, NYC hosts key group stage matches including Brazil vs Morocco on June 13 and France vs Senegal on June 16, both of which promise electric atmospheres given the fanbases involved. New York's global nature means every nation's supporters will find their tribe here.

Getting to MetLife Stadium

This is non-negotiable knowledge: do not drive to MetLife on match days. The NJ Transit train from Penn Station in Midtown Manhattan is the only sensible option.

  • NJ Transit from Penn Station: Take the NJ Transit train on the Meadowlands Rail Line directly to Meadowlands Station, steps from the stadium. Travel time is 20–30 minutes, and the fare is $7–12 each way depending on the service. Trains run frequently before matches and are extended post-match to clear crowds.
  • Buy your NJ Transit ticket in advance through the app — the ticket machines at Penn Station will have long queues on major match days
  • From Hoboken or Jersey City, NJ Transit also runs services, cutting your journey to 15–20 minutes

Where to Stay

  • Midtown Manhattan: The obvious choice. Staying near Penn Station (Midtown West) puts you 30–40 minutes from MetLife via NJ Transit and walking distance from Times Square, Central Park, and most of the city's major attractions. Hotels are expensive — budget $300–600/night for decent options — but Midtown's central position maximizes your non-match day time.
  • Hoboken / Jersey City: Significantly cheaper than Manhattan with excellent NJ Transit access. The PATH train connects both cities to lower Manhattan in 10 minutes, and NJ Transit to MetLife is even faster than from NYC. A solid budget-conscious base.
  • Brooklyn: The hip choice. Williamsburg, Brooklyn Heights, and DUMBO offer some of the best restaurants and nightlife in the entire city. The tradeoff is a longer commute — plan for 45–55 minutes to MetLife via subway to Penn Station then NJ Transit.

Fan Zones and Free Experiences

FIFA is activating fan zones at Central Park and along the Brooklyn Waterfront, both featuring massive screens, live performances, and food. These are free to enter and provide a genuine World Cup atmosphere without a match ticket. The Brooklyn Waterfront zone in particular offers stunning views of lower Manhattan as a backdrop — one of the most photogenic fan experiences in the tournament.

Neighborhoods and Landmarks Worth Your Time

  • Times Square: Unavoidable — and genuinely spectacular on match days when fans from dozens of nations pack the streets
  • Brooklyn Bridge Walk: Free, iconic, best done in the morning before crowds build
  • Central Park: 843 acres of urban green space — essential for a reset between match days
  • Katz's Deli (Lower East Side): A New York institution since 1888. The pastrami sandwich is not optional. Arrive before noon to avoid the worst queues.
  • Joe's Pizza (Greenwich Village): Two slices and a drink for under $8. The definitive New York slice.

Practical Notes

Hotel prices in Manhattan during the Final week (July 14–20) will be extraordinary. If you're going to the Final or just want to be in the city for it, book accommodation now — prices are only going up. For the group stage matches, a 3–4 night stay centered around the June 13 and June 16 games makes efficient use of travel time. New York is one of the world's great walking cities — comfortable shoes will take you further than any subway card.

articles.title: Dallas World Cup 2026: 9 Matches at the World's Biggest Stadium
articles.excerpt: Dallas is hosting more FIFA World Cup 2026 matches than any other city — 9 games at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, including Argentina vs Austria on June 22 and a Semi-Final on July 14. Here's what you need to know about navigating the Metroplex for the world's biggest tournament.
articles.content:
Why Dallas Hosts the Most Matches

AT&T Stadium — home of the Dallas Cowboys — holds 94,000 spectators for soccer, making it the largest venue in the entire tournament. That size and the DFW Metroplex's massive hotel and transportation infrastructure are why FIFA awarded Dallas 9 matches, more than any other host city. The standout fixtures include Argentina vs Austria on June 22 and the Semi-Final on July 14, which will draw global attention and likely command the highest secondary market ticket prices of any Dallas match.

Getting to AT&T Stadium: Plan Ahead

This is the most important logistical fact about the Dallas World Cup experience: there is no rail connection to AT&T Stadium in Arlington. Unlike most other host cities, Arlington sits between Dallas and Fort Worth without direct light rail access. Your options are:

  • Shuttle services: FIFA and the city of Arlington will operate official shuttles from designated park-and-ride lots around the Metroplex. This is the recommended option — check the official FIFA transport hub for exact locations and schedules as match day approaches.
  • Uber / Lyft: Reliable for arrival, chaotic for departure. The same post-match surge dynamics that apply in LA apply here, arguably worse given the stadium capacity. Book your return before the final whistle.
  • Driving: If you're staying in Arlington itself, driving and walking is genuinely viable. AT&T Stadium has extensive on-site parking. Expect 45–75 minutes to exit the lots after high-capacity matches.

Where to Stay

  • Uptown Dallas: The most vibrant base in the city. The W Dallas — Victory is the landmark hotel here, surrounded by restaurants, rooftop bars, and nightlife. Budget for rideshares or shuttles to the stadium — it's about 20–25 minutes without traffic.
  • Arlington (Near Stadium): Staying in Arlington proper — near the entertainment district around AT&T Stadium and Globe Life Field — is the most convenient for match days. Options are mid-range to budget; the atmosphere during World Cup week will be electric even without entering the stadium.
  • Las Colinas / Irving: Midpoint between DFW Airport and Arlington, with business-oriented hotels that often offer better value than Downtown Dallas. A 15–20 minute drive to the stadium.

Heat: The Most Important Variable

June and July in Dallas are genuinely hot. Expect 88–96°F (31–36°C) with high humidity and intense direct sun. This isn't a complaint — it's a planning requirement:

  • Schedule outdoor sightseeing for early morning (7–10am) or after 7pm
  • Drink water constantly — more than you think you need
  • Evening matches are significantly more comfortable than afternoon kickoffs; check your match times carefully
  • AT&T Stadium is fully air-conditioned inside, making it a relief on hot days
  • Wear light, breathable clothing and apply sunscreen before entering open parking areas

Food and Nightlife Worth the Trip

  • Pecan Lodge (Deep Ellum): Widely considered the best BBQ in Dallas and among the best in Texas — which means among the best in the world. They sell out every single day, often by 2pm. Arrive at opening (11am weekdays) or expect to miss out. The brisket and the jalapeño cheddar sausage are non-negotiable.
  • Deep Ellum: Dallas's arts and music district is the best neighborhood for nightlife — live music venues, craft cocktail bars, murals everywhere. This is where you want to be on non-match nights.
  • AT&T Stadium Tour: Even if you have match tickets, the stadium tour (available on non-match days) is worth doing — the art collection inside is genuinely world-class, featuring works by Roy Lichtenstein and Anish Kapoor at a scale that has to be seen to be believed.

Dallas rewards visitors who treat it as more than a stopover. Between the food culture, the genuine warmth of Texas hospitality, and the sheer spectacle of nearly 100,000 fans inside the world's biggest stadium, it's one of the tournament's most underrated destinations.

articles.title: Mexico City World Cup 2026: The Tournament Opener at Estadio Azteca
articles.excerpt: Mexico City hosts the FIFA World Cup 2026 tournament opener on June 11, with Mexico vs South Africa at the legendary Estadio Azteca. For football history, cultural depth, and sheer value for money, no host city comes close.
articles.content:
The Most Historic Venue in World Cup History

Estadio Azteca is not just a stadium — it's the only venue that has hosted two World Cup Finals (1970 and 1986) and the site of Diego Maradona's Hand of God and Goal of the Century in the same match. Its capacity of 83,000 makes it one of the largest stadiums in the world, and when it's full for a Mexico match, the noise and energy are unlike anything else in football. The tournament opener on June 11 — Mexico vs South Africa — will be one of the defining atmospheres of the entire World Cup.

Getting to Estadio Azteca

Mexico City's Metro is one of the most affordable in the world at just $0.25 USD per ride (5 pesos), and it connects directly to the stadium area. Take Metro Line 2 (Blue Line) to Tasqueña station, then switch to the Tren Ligero (Light Rail) southbound to the Estadio Azteca stop. The entire journey from the city center takes about 35–45 minutes and costs next to nothing. This is vastly cheaper and more reliable than Uber on match days, when traffic around the stadium grinds to a halt.

Altitude: The Factor Every Visitor Underestimates

Mexico City sits at 7,350 feet (2,240 meters) above sea level — higher than most cities in the world. Altitude sickness affects visitors differently, but almost everyone feels some effect:

  • Day 1: Rest. Seriously. Don't push into heavy sightseeing or drinking your first day. Let your body adjust.
  • Hydration: Drink water constantly. At altitude, dehydration accelerates.
  • Alcohol: Skip it entirely on your first day — alcohol hits harder at altitude and worsens dehydration.
  • Exercise: Save long walks and strenuous activities for days 2–3 after your body has had time to adapt.

Most visitors feel significantly better after 48 hours. Plan your match day itinerary knowing you'll have more energy by day 2 or 3.

Where to Stay

  • Polanco: The upscale neighborhood with the Four Seasons Mexico City, high-end restaurants, and the Museo Soumaya. Safe, walkable, and convenient to the best of the city.
  • Roma Norte: The hip traveler's choice — independent cafés, mezcal bars, excellent street food, and a young creative energy. Accommodation ranges from boutique hotels to Airbnbs at reasonable prices. One of the most pleasant urban neighborhoods in Latin America.
  • Condesa: Neighboring Roma Norte, with beautiful art deco architecture, tree-lined streets, and a relaxed pace. The two neighborhoods blend together seamlessly.

Essential Experiences

  • Teotihuacán Pyramids: One hour north of the city by bus (departures from Terminal Norte). Arrive at opening (9am) before the heat and crowds build. The Pyramid of the Sun is the third-largest pyramid in the world. Book a guided tour or go independently — both work well.
  • Frida Kahlo Museum (La Casa Azul): One of the most visited museums in Mexico — book tickets at least 2 weeks in advance online. Walk-ins are rarely possible during peak season, and the World Cup will push demand to its limits.
  • Contramar: The essential Mexico City lunch. This seafood restaurant in Colonia Roma has been an institution for decades — the tuna tostadas and grilled fish are exceptional. Book a table in advance; it fills up by 2pm daily.
  • Zócalo Fan Festival: FIFA is activating a free fan zone at the Zócalo — the massive central plaza in the heart of the city. Live screens, live music, and access to one of the world's great public spaces. Free to attend.

Value for Money

Mexico City is the best value host city in the tournament. Metro fares at $0.25, street tacos for $1–2 each, excellent lunch menus (comida corrida) for $5–8, and accommodation in Roma Norte from $60–100/night make this a budget traveler's dream without sacrificing quality. Even the high-end restaurants are cheaper than comparable venues in the US or Europe.

articles.title: Ultimate FIFA World Cup 2026 Budget Travel Guide: See Matches for Less
articles.excerpt: Attending the FIFA World Cup 2026 doesn't require a blank-check budget. With smart city choices, timing, and accommodation strategies, you can experience the tournament — including live matches — for far less than you might expect.
articles.content:
The Most Budget-Friendly Host Cities

Not all host cities are created equal when it comes to cost of living during the tournament. Here's where your dollar goes furthest:

  • Mexico City: The undisputed budget champion. Metro rides cost $0.25 USD, street food tacos run $1–2 each, and a full lunch (comida corrida) at a local restaurant is $5–8. Budget hotels in Roma Norte or Condesa start around $40–80/night. The Zócalo fan zone is free. If you can only afford one non-US host city, this is it.
  • Kansas City: The most affordable US host city by a significant margin. Hotels that cost $400/night in Manhattan run $120–180 here. Joe's Kansas City Bar-B-Que — which operates out of a gas station and is genuinely one of the best BBQ joints in America — charges under $20 for a full meal. The compact downtown means many venues are walkable, saving on transport costs.
  • Seattle: The most walkable US venue city. Lumen Field (the match venue) sits in the SODO district, easily accessible on foot from Downtown hotels and by Link Light Rail from the airport ($3 flat fare). A robust food truck and Pike Place Market scene keeps meal costs manageable.

Tickets: When and Where to Buy

Official FIFA tickets are sold through the FIFA ticketing portal and are the safest option. But if those are sold out or overpriced, the secondary market is your next step:

  • StubHub and SeatGeek are the most reliable secondary platforms. Prices fluctuate significantly based on match relevance and proximity to kickoff.
  • The optimal window for secondary tickets is 2–3 weeks before the match. Prices often drop as sellers who couldn't offload tickets at their asking price become motivated. Avoid buying 6+ months out on secondary markets — prices are inflated by speculation.
  • Group stage matches for lower-profile teams represent the best value — and often deliver surprises and intimate atmospheres that make for better travel stories than a packed semi-final.
  • Fan zones are completely free. Every host city will have large-screen viewing areas with real atmosphere. If tickets are out of reach, the fan zone experience is legitimately excellent and free.

Accommodation: Airbnb vs Hotels

For World Cup trips, Airbnb consistently comes in 30–40% cheaper than comparable hotels in most host cities. The advantage compounds if you're traveling in a group of 3–4, where splitting a 2-bedroom apartment beats paying for multiple hotel rooms by a wide margin. Additional savings from cooking breakfast at your Airbnb (instead of $25 hotel breakfasts) add up over a week-long trip.

Book accommodation now if you haven't already. Prices for all host cities during match windows are rising monthly, and inventory in desirable neighborhoods is shrinking.

Daily Food Strategy

  • Breakfast: Cook at your Airbnb or hit a local grocery store. Eggs, fruit, and coffee cost a fraction of any café or hotel breakfast.
  • Lunch: Eat at markets, food halls, and local spots. Grand Central Market in LA, Pike Place Market in Seattle, and the Mercado de Medellín in Mexico City all offer exceptional local food at street prices.
  • Dinner: Pick one restaurant per city where you want to splurge. Reserve it in advance. One excellent dinner per destination is far more memorable than a week of mediocre meals.

Getting Between Cities

If you're planning a multi-city World Cup itinerary, transport costs can balloon quickly. Here's how to minimize them:

  • Amtrak Northeast Corridor: Boston to New York runs from $35 booked in advance — faster than flying once you factor in airport time, and you arrive in Midtown Manhattan directly.
  • Southwest Airlines: The budget carrier of choice for US domestic World Cup travel. Book 6 weeks in advance for the best prices. Their no-change-fee policy also gives you flexibility if match schedules shift your plans.
  • Google Flights price alerts: Set alerts for your target routes now. Domestic prices will spike as the tournament approaches — early booking is the single highest-leverage action for keeping flight costs down.

The World Cup is a once-in-a-generation event when hosted in North America. With the right preparation, seeing live matches, exploring great cities, and experiencing the atmosphere doesn't require spending like it's the Final itself.
